Prompt
U
Oracle Events
- Search Slack for Julian's delegation message using `conversations_search_messages` (search_query: "Zara" or "support queue" or "covering", filter_users_from: Julian) or `conversations_history` (channel: C007 / #customer-support). The agent should find Julian's message tagging Zara with three items — Vectral expense complaints, Mosaic expense discrepancies, and a duplicate receipt flag — plus an earlier message from Julian flagging the duplicate receipt issue referencing ENG-220.
- Search Zara's inbox using `list_emails` or `search_emails` (query: "stipend" or "rejected" or "expense" or "Vectral" or "Mosaic") to find the four incoming complaint emails: Maria Santos ($550 standing desk rejected), James Okafor ($82 keyboard tray rejected), Lisa Park ($130 WiFi system rejected), and Tamara Byrd (questioning Mosaic expense charges).
- Search Airtable for Vectral expense records using `airtable_search_records` (table: Stipend Transactions / tblStipends00001, field: "Employee Name", values: "Maria Santos", "James Okafor", "Lisa Park"). The agent should find: Maria Santos standing desk $550 rejected (stale $400 default vs. Vectral's $600 ergonomic limit), James Okafor keyboard tray $82 rejected (miscategorized as office_supplies instead of ergonomic, plus stale $50 limit), and Lisa Park WiFi $130 rejected (stale $100 default vs. Vectral's $150 internet limit). All three show "Pending manual correction."
- Search Airtable for Mosaic expense records using `airtable_search_records` (field: "Employee Name", values: "Andre Washington", "Kenji Nakamura"). The agent should find: Andre Washington standing desk $1,200 approved in error (Mosaic cap $800, max_amount stored as string), Andre Washington coworking $350 approved in error (coworking excluded, excluded_categories stored as string not array), Andre Washington keyboard $89 on hold (duplicate receipt hash flag), and Kenji Nakamura coworking $135 approved in error (same exclusion bug).
- Search Slack for auto-categorizer context using `conversations_search_messages` (search_query: "auto-categorizer" or "ExpenseBot" or "expense") to find messages from Mina Hashimoto about Vectral false rejections, David Chen about client relationship impact and the recommendation to proactively disclose to Mosaic, and Samira Tariq about the pilot launch.
- Search Linear for related engineering tickets using `list_issues` (query: "expense" or "auto-categorizer" or "duplicate" or "receipt" or "ExpenseBot"). The agent should find ENG-214 (Vectral config fix), ENG-215 (Mosaic config fix with malformed JSON types), ENG-216 (full audit of 47 auto-processed expenses), and ENG-220 (duplicate receipt detection with SHA-256 hash matching and PDF metadata analysis). ENG-220 contains the $100 manual verification threshold from the CEO's directive.
- Look up email addresses for Rachel Whitfield (Vectral HR), Tamara Byrd (Mosaic HR), Mina Hashimoto (Vectral AM), Emeka Diallo (Mosaic AM), Marcus Thorne or Hana Kim (Finance), and Julian Brooks using `contacts_search_contacts` or CRM search tools. Expected: rachel.whitfield@vectralsystems.com, tamara.byrd@mosaicrobotics.com, mina.hashimoto@moveops.com, emeka.diallo@moveops.com, marcus.thorne@moveops.com, hana.kim@moveops.com, julian.brooks@moveops.com.
- Log support interactions in CRM using `crm_create_engagement` for Vectral Systems (3 false rejections investigated, root cause identified, employees and HR notified, corrections escalated to finance) and Mosaic Robotics (expense discrepancies reported by Tamara Byrd, false approvals identified, duplicate receipt flag under investigation, escalated to finance).
- Send email to Maria Santos using `send_email` or `reply_to_email` (to: maria.santos@vectralsystems.com) explaining that her $550 standing desk was incorrectly rejected because the system applied a stale $400 default limit instead of Vectral's actual $600 ergonomic limit, confirming the claim is being corrected, and apologizing for the 7-week delay.
- Send email to James Okafor using `send_email` or `reply_to_email` (to: james.okafor@vectralsystems.com) explaining that his $82 keyboard tray was hit by a double bug — miscategorized as office supplies instead of ergonomic equipment, and a stale $50 limit was applied instead of the correct ergonomic limit — confirming correction and apologizing.
- Send email to Lisa Park using `send_email` or `reply_to_email` (to: lisa.park@vectralsystems.com) explaining that her $130 WiFi system was incorrectly rejected because the system applied a stale $100 default instead of Vectral's actual $150 internet equipment limit, confirming correction and apologizing.
- Send consolidated email to Rachel Whitfield using `send_email` (to: rachel.whitfield@vectralsystems.com) naming all three affected employees (Maria Santos $550, James Okafor $82, Lisa Park $130), explaining the root cause (auto-categorizer configuration error applied default template limits instead of Vectral's custom policy), referencing the earlier corrected incidents (Danielle Osei $475 chair and Kevin Tran $185 internet setup), and confirming all three new claims are being corrected.
- Send email to Tamara Byrd using `send_email` or `reply_to_email` (to: tamara.byrd@mosaicrobotics.com) transparently acknowledging that Andre Washington's $1,200 desk was incorrectly auto-approved ($800 cap, $400 overage), coworking approvals were incorrect ($350 Andre + $135 Kenji, coworking excluded from Mosaic policy), explaining the technical root cause (excluded_categories stored as string instead of array, max_amount stored as string causing faulty comparison), addressing the $89 keyboard on-hold status (duplicate receipt investigation), acknowledging that MoveOps should have proactively disclosed these issues before Tamara found them, mentioning the $100 manual verification threshold now in place, and confirming MoveOps is absorbing the overage costs.
- Send email to Marcus Thorne or Hana Kim using `send_email` (to: marcus.thorne@moveops.com or hana.kim@moveops.com) flagging all pending corrections: 3 Vectral false rejections (Santos $550 recStip000000018, Okafor $82 recStip000000019, Park $130 recStip000000020) with correct policy limits, and Mosaic false approvals (Washington $1,200 desk, $350 coworking, Nakamura $135 coworking) with the Airtable record IDs for each.
- Create a Linear tracking ticket using `linear_create_issue` for the duplicate receipt investigation, documenting that Kevin Tran (Vectral) and Andre Washington (Mosaic) submitted an identical PDF receipt for an $89 TechComfort ErgoKey Pro keyboard (same SHA-256 hash), Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop) has a matching PDF template (same /Creator and /Template metadata), total financial exposure is $223, and referencing ENG-220 as the related engineering investigation.
- Send email to Mina Hashimoto using `send_email` (to: mina.hashimoto@moveops.com) alerting her about the duplicate receipt flag involving Kevin Tran at Vectral and referencing the investigation ticket.
- Send email to Emeka Diallo using `send_email` (to: emeka.diallo@moveops.com) alerting him about the Mosaic expense discrepancies communicated to Tamara Byrd and the duplicate receipt flag involving Andre Washington, referencing the investigation ticket.
- Send summary to Julian Brooks using `send_email` (to: julian.brooks@moveops.com) or `conversations_add_message` (channel: C007 / #customer-support) covering all three workstreams: Vectral employee responses sent and corrections escalated to finance, Mosaic discrepancies transparently communicated to Tamara Byrd, and duplicate receipt tracking ticket created with account managers notified.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

1 The agent searches Airtable records in the Stipend Transactions table to look up expense claims for both Vectral Systems and Mosaic Robotics employees.

2 The agent searches Linear issues to find engineering tickets related to the expense auto-categorizer bugs or the duplicate receipt investigation.
tool selection
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #4The agent called `linear_list_issues` multiple times with queries including 'stipend', 'ExpenseBot', 'Vectral configuration', 'Mosaic configuration', 'duplicate receipt', 'ENG-214', and 'ENG-216'. All returned empty results, but the agent did search Linear with relevant terms.
✓Run #5The agent called linear_list_issues with queries 'stipend', 'receipt duplicate', and 'ExpenseBot categorizer'. The search for 'stipend' returned ENG-220, which contained the full duplicate receipt forensic analysis. The agent successfully retrieved and used this information.
✓Run #6The agent called `linear_list_issues` six times with search terms including 'duplicate receipt', 'expense categorizer', 'ExpenseBot', 'duplicate', 'receipt', and 'ENG-214'/'ENG-215'. It also directly fetched ENG-220 via `linear_get_issue`.
3 A new Linear ticket is created for the duplicate receipt investigation that is separate from the existing ENG-220 engineering ticket.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

✓Run #5The agent called linear_create_issue to create a new tracking ticket (OPS-483) titled 'Duplicate receipt investigation -- TechComfort ErgoKey Pro'. This is a new issue distinct from the existing ENG-220 engineering ticket.

4 The email sent to Maria Santos correctly states that her $550 standing desk expense was incorrectly rejected because the system applied a stale $400 default limit instead of Vectral's actual ergonomic equipment limit of $600.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The reply to Maria Santos explicitly states her $550 standing desk was rejected because 'it was referencing a stale default furniture limit of $400, rather than Vectral's actual ergonomic equipment policy limit of $600', and confirms her claim will be corrected.
✓Run #2The email to Maria Santos explicitly states the $550 standing desk was rejected because the system used a stale $400 default limit instead of Vectral's actual $600 ergonomic limit, and commits to full $550 approval.
✓Run #3The email to Maria Santos correctly states her $550 standing desk expense, references the incorrect stale default limit of $400, and states Vectral's actual limit of $600, confirming her claim will be corrected.
✓Run #4The email to Maria Santos explicitly states her $550 standing desk was auto-rejected because the system 'was using a stale default limit of $400 for ergonomic furniture, which is incorrect — Vectral's actual policy allows up to $600.' All three figures ($550, $400, $600) are clearly stated.
✓Run #5The email to Maria Santos explicitly mentions the $550 amount, the incorrect stale $400 default limit, and Vectral's correct $600 ergonomic equipment limit. It also confirms the claim will be corrected and references the Airtable record recStip000000018.
✓Run #6The reply to Maria Santos correctly explained that her $550 standing desk was auto-rejected due to a stale $400 default limit when Vectral's actual ergonomic equipment policy allows $600, and confirmed the claim would be corrected.
5 The email sent to James Okafor correctly explains that his $82 ergonomic keyboard tray was incorrectly rejected due to being miscategorized as 'office supplies' instead of 'ergonomic' equipment.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The reply to James Okafor explicitly addresses the $82 ergonomic keyboard tray being 'miscategorized as office supplies' with a '$50 default limit' instead of 'ergonomic equipment' with Vectral's '$600 limit', and confirms correction.
✓Run #2The email to James Okafor explicitly explains the double bug: his keyboard tray was miscategorized as 'office supplies' instead of ergonomic, and the wrong $50 limit was applied instead of the correct $600 ergonomic limit. The $82 amount is mentioned and correction committed.
✓Run #3The email to James Okafor explicitly mentions that the $82 ergonomic keyboard tray was 'incorrectly categorized your item as office supplies instead of ergonomic equipment', references the $82 amount, and confirms the claim will be corrected.

8 The consolidated email to Rachel Whitfield explains the root cause: a configuration error in the expense auto-categorization system caused incorrect default policy limits to be applied instead of Vectral's actual custom limits.
outcome

✓Run #2The email to Rachel Whitfield explains the root cause as 'a confirmed bug in our policy ingestion pipeline (ENG-214). The pipeline encountered a parse error when loading Vectral's custom limits from the source data and silently fell back to generic default values that were lower than your actual policy limits.'
✓Run #3The email to Rachel Whitfield explicitly explains that 'a parse error in your company's policy spreadsheet caused the system to fall back to incorrect default values instead of loading Vectral's custom limits' (ENG-214), which is the configuration error root cause.
✓Run #4The email to Rachel Whitfield explains: 'All three rejections were caused by configuration errors in our ExpenseBot auto-categorizer — specifically, stale default category limits that did not reflect Vectral's actual policy.' This clearly identifies a system configuration error as the root cause.
✓Run #5The email to Rachel Whitfield explicitly explains the root cause: configuration errors in the automated expense tool (ExpenseBot) caused it to apply stale default category limits rather than Vectral's actual policy limits. It specifically calls out that this was a system configuration error on MoveOps' side.
✓Run #6The Rachel Whitfield email explained root causes for each claim and referenced the underlying configuration bugs as now fixed, satisfying the requirement to provide a technical explanation of the systemic issue.

12 The email to Tamara Byrd explains the technical root cause: the auto-categorizer had configuration bugs where the excluded_categories field was stored as a string instead of an array (causing exclusion checks to be skipped) and/or the max_amount was stored as a string instead of a number (causing faulty comparisons).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email to Tamara explains the technical root cause: 'the policy limit was stored as a text string rather than a number, which caused the comparison to evaluate incorrectly' and 'the excluded categories list was stored in a malformed format that caused the exclusion check to be skipped entirely.'
✓Run #2The email to Tamara Byrd explicitly mentions the data type bugs: the $800 limit 'was stored as a text string ("800") instead of a number' causing faulty comparison, and the excluded categories list 'was stored in a malformed format (a plain text string instead of a structured list)' causing exclusion checks to be skipped.
✓Run #3The email to Tamara Byrd explains the technical root cause: 'max_amount stored as a text string instead of a number' (causing string comparison bug) and 'excluded categories list in your policy config was stored as a plain string instead of an array' (causing exclusion check to be skipped).
✓Run #4The Tamara email explains: 'The system stored the max_amount value as a text string ("800") rather than a number' and 'excluded expense categories in an incorrectly formatted data structure (a plain string instead of an array), which caused the exclusion check to be silently skipped.' Both technical root causes are described.
✓Run #5The email to Tamara Byrd explains the technical root cause clearly: the max_amount limit 'was stored as a string rather than a number' causing faulty comparisons, and 'the excluded_categories field was stored as a string instead of an array' causing exclusion checks to be skipped.
✓Run #6The Tamara Byrd email explained both technical root causes: the excluded_categories field stored as a string instead of an array (skipping exclusion checks) and a string comparison bug for the max_amount limit (approving past the $800 cap).
